GURRENT AFFAIRS,

Mr. McVeagh, M.P., is to be congratulated. On
many occasions he asked unsuccessfully in the British
Parliament, for a retwrn in the following form :—“Re-
turn setting forth the name, age, length of service, and
place of education of each salaried official in various
~ Government, departments in Ireland.” It appears that
* recently he caught the enemy napping, and the request
for this return was granted without opposition. There
were developments when the enemy roused himself up
from his nap. Mr. McVeagh was requested to withdraw

request for the return; very properly he refused to

2 ng of the sort. It was notified then that Mr,

Long would attempt to muzzle the mad dog that Mr.
McVeagh had unloosed from the ascendancy kennel.
Tortunately there must be a discussion upon Mr. Long's
new muzzling order, and that will be informing and in-
teresting, even if, eventually, a muzzle is placed on
the obnoxious Irish wolf dog who advisedly barks at
bigots. We, and we presume Mr. McVeagh also, are
not interested in the length of service of the spongers
on Dublin Castle, but we would like to know where the
spongers were educated. After all, it does not need ‘a
Sherlock Holmes to decide the colour of a jobber who
was dragged up in a “Blue School!” We hope that
Mr. McVeagh will succeed in obtaining this wholesome
and informing return; if he does not succeed we hope
that the debate on the motion to muzzle the unruly
“Idolatrous” wolf dog will not be passed until the wolf
dog has kept the neighbours awake by loud and pro-
longed barking and some biting.

There is a man by the name of Alderman Hadden in
Wexford town, and he has recently taught the  Idola-
tors™ of that place a lesson. He meant to teach them a
lesson, but it is possible that the timely lesson that they
have been taught is not exactly the one that the “saved”
Wexford Alderman had intended for them. Alderman
Hadden runs a large drapery establishment in Wexford,
and does a large—if not, indeed, the largest—trade
with “TIdolators” in the town; and this notwithstand-
ing that we hear he is rather exclusive in the matter of
the “colour ” of his employees; and that he signed the
anti-Home Rule petition in Wexford in '86. The intro-
duction to this interesting Wexford story of a loyal and
pious rebellion may be best told by quoting a few sen-
tences from our contemporary, the Enniscorthy Echo, of
March 24th : —“ We are glad to note that the suggestion
thrown out by Mr. T. P. Gill to the Wexford Hat Com-
pany in the Town Hall on the occasion of his visit here
last week with regard to sending an exhibit to the Lan-
guage Procession in Dublin on last Sunday was adopted
in a most practical manner. The directors of the com-
pany ordered several makes and patterns of their hats
to be sent on by mail train on Saturday evening, and
these, together with a ‘ blocking’ machine and two ope-
rators to work it, duly arrived in Dublin on Saturday
night. In the procession on Language Day one of the
most prominent displays in the industrial section was
the large dray bearing the above-mentioned articles, and
the two hat-makers, Messts. Wren and Murphy, making
new ones as they went along. It commanded a great
deal of admiration, and we are sure it ‘will not be with-
out its good results.” So far, so good. But Alderman
Hadden, Unionist and thriver upon “Idolatrous” Wex-
ford trade, was shocked at the base perfidy of his brother
directors.

Shortly after the Procession the following letter from
him appeared in' the congenial columns of the Zrish
Z'dmes :—* Sir—I learn with much regret that during
my absence in: London a majority of my brother direo-
tors of the Wexford Hat Company, Ltd., decided te
have it represented at the Gaelic League Demonstration
on Sunday. It is not for me to sit in judgment upon
my friends, who have, I am sure, acted in all good faith.
At the same time I seek the courtesy of your columns
to enable me to dissociate myself from their action on
this occasion, as it is to my mind a distinct offence
against what I conceive to be the proper observance of
the Lord’s Day.—George Hadden, Springfield, Wex-
ford.” Since this letter appeared this pious Alderman
has resigned his seat on the Board of the Wexford
Hat Company; this tolerant gentleman could not, we
suppose, sit on a Board the majority of which had com-
mitted such an awful crime. Perhaps Brother Gould-

98

ing and all the rest of the “saved” on the railways will
Tesign their jobs if there is any more railway traffic on
Sundays! How can Brother Goulding and the sancti-
monious Mr. Ormsby, for instance, reconcile the running
of Great Sourface trains on a Sunday when Alderman
Hadden, of Wexford, is shocked ab the Hat Exhibit in
2 Procession held on a Sunday? We trust that the con-
sciences of Brother Goulding, Mr. Ormsby, and a great
many others of that ilk will compel] them to resign their
‘highly-paid jobs! Iet them show that they are every
bib.as “saved” as their Wexford brother. This Wex-
ford Alderman announced to his brother directors of the
hat factory that he would resign unless they pledged
themselves to be good boys, and not allow their Hat
Exhibit to go processioning on any futuro Sundays; his
‘brother directors laughed, and Hadden. accordingly re-
signed. Now, if Hadden caunot reconcile it with his
conscience to sit; on this Board, how can. he reconcile his
<conscience to trade with “Idolators” in Wexford who
“run trains and cars, and would carry out a procession
<on any Sunday of the year? Perhaps he. will now teach
the rebelly Papists, Croppy boys, of Wexford a further
lesson by shutting up his shop, and so let. low-down
Croppy drapers divide his business between them. No
doubt the hat factory, a new industry, will, with the
thelp of the extra support that will naturally rally round
it ‘as a result of Hadden’s action, survive even the cala-
ity of his resignation.

"We learn from a correspondent that in a certain Wes-
tern town the Railway Hotel is he most imposing struc-
fure one sees on entering its busy little station. It is
only right to say that besides borrowing its name this
house has no other connection with the railway company.
AVhen the visitor on entering is shewn his room his atten-
%ion is at once attracted by a tablet on the wall bearing
the inscription :—* Only soap used in this
Hotel”—a much-puffed London variety being indicated ;
and an examination of the soap itself shews it to be
branded “Supplied specially for use in this Hotel.” TIs
this a new or a very old device of the enterprising
foreigner to oust his native rivals? It is, how-
-ever, to be assumed that the makers of this foreign
variety of soap have no particular interest in washing
gratuitously the patrons of this inn. What, then, is
the consideration for this wanton boycott of native in-
dustry which the- proprietress of this establishment—
thoughtlessly, perhaps, but unpatriotically—indulges in ?

Captain Salwey, the ex-Coastguard, has happily
ridded this country of his zealous presence. It will be
remembered this was the man who, some months ago,

. gained some notoriety by dropping tracts in the Cork
Post Office. Recently his zeal for saving “Idolators”
brought him into a Catholic Church at Kinsale, and he
attempted to harangue the congregation. On last
Saturday he appeared in a Court of Law, and his solici-
tor having given an undertaking that Salwey would rid
this “Idolatrous” country of his presence, the prosecution
was adjourned sine die. We trust that Salwey is now in
England, and we hope he will stay there or clse go to
Scotland ; in either place he will find plenty of scope
for his energies if he has the interests of morality at
heart. .

The report for 1904 for the Diocese of Ferns of St.
Patrick’s anti-Treating League gives us an opportunity
of bringing this beneficent League before the notice of
@ur readers again. Our readers will be interested in
the following paragraphs under the sub-heading
““ Autumn Crusade ” :—“ Last October a circular letter
from our committee was sent, to all the clergy who have
introduced the League into their parishes, suggesting an
Autumn Crusade on the lines of that held the previous
year. In response to this circular renewal meetings
were held, and, we are happy to say, with excellent re-
sults throughout the four Deaneries of the Diocese.
Bighteen such meetings with special sermons were held

* in the Wexford Deanery, thirteen in Gorey, eleven in
Ehniscorthy, and ten in New Ross. This does not in-
- clude the churches where the pledge was privately re-

THE LEADER.

APpRIL 8, 1905.

newed by the local priest himself. The mcetiqgs, in
most instances, were well attended, the congregation, in
many cases, even exceeding that of the previous year.
The priests of neighbouring parishes co-operated with
one anobher in those renewals this time on a larger scale
than even previously. This is a feature of the Autumn
Crusade we heartily commend. It is the unanimous
opinion of the committee that these periodic Crusades
are very valuable in the interests of temperance gener-
ally, as well as being almost vital (oy the 'mmmiena‘nce of
our anti-Treating Movement.” It is claimed in the re-
port for the Diocese of Ferns that the anti-Treating
League “has checled and largely discredited the deep-
rooted treating system. It has released all well-meaning
men from a species of social thraldom from which they
could not individually and of themselves escape.”

The main principles of the anti-Treating League are
three:—(1) anti-treating; (2) the practice of strict
temperance by all members ; and (3) the creation of pub-
lic opinion against drunkenness and making' others
drunk. There is no sensible man in Ireland who does
not see that the ¢ convention” of publichouse treating is
one of the great feeders of that terrible drain of some
£14,000,000 a year that is collected by Bung from a
green people. Bung is not a Dark Brother; he adver-
tises and places alluring fairy lamps in his caves. The
slum residences of so many of the poor lielp to send many
of their occupants to the gaudilylighted saloons of
Bung; and the besotted victims of Bung, who collects
some £14,000,000 a year from this poor country, in turn
lelp to drag people from decent and humble homes into
the worst slums.  Bung is altogether a disproportionate
interest—commercially and socially—in this country ;
it is abnormal that the runners of grog-shops should be
one of the chief, if not the chief, constituents of such
middle-class as we have in Ireland. Bung has a strong
organisation; the flowing tide unfortunately, to a great
extent, is with him still, and, in addition to all this, his
revenue is swelled by foolish people who force drink in
publichouses upon acquaintances who do not want it; a
perverted notion of hospitality pumps money into Bung’s
till and into the Government Exchequer. But all these
and similar considerations are commonplaces to our rea-
ders now, and are accepted by them. The problem is
to kill, as far as possible, the public-house treating
custom. TFor that purpose an organisation is requisite,
and the organisation exists. From a paper read by the
Hon. Sec. of the anti-Treating League, Father John J.
Rossiter, M.SS., of Enniscorthy, at the Maynooth Union
last year, we quote the following:—* Perhaps this is
the best place to state that the Committee of Priests who
formed this League never for a moment conceived the
thought of organising it on the lines of a Church Con-
fraternity. They did not consider this laborious method
of working it either necessary or desirable. Probably
we are all fairly well satisfied that we have got confra-
ternities enough already.  Yet organisation of some
kind is of vital necessity. But what better organisation
for such a purpose as ours can be invented by man than
the splendid organisation ready made to our hand and
spread like a neb over the country—the perfect organisa-
tion of the Church itself. With all Ireland divided into
dioceses and sub-divided into parishes, and with all the
people assembling once a week for Sunday’s Mass and
Sunday Sermon, what further organisation for the pro-
pagation of any great moral crusade can we priests need
if we only conspire to use for the purpose owr magnifi-
cent opportunities? Taking this view, we rely on the
Sunday Sermon as the great means of propagating and
sustaining the anti-Treating League. In addition to
this, we require just as much in the way of parochial
organisation as will make the members a distinct body
in the parish known to the priest as members and known
to onoe another, and sharply distinguished from non-
members. For this purpose, and also for the purpose of
solemnly renewing the pledge, the committee appointed
two days in the year for the special meetings of members_z.
Granted certain conditions, the committee believed this
is the simple form' of’ organisation that would yield the
maximum of good results with the minimum expendi-

APRIL 8, 1905.

ture of additional time and energy.”  We think the
time is opportune for what we may call a spurt in the
anti-Treating Campaign. There was great enthusiasm
and much activity when the League was established
- SOIe years ago ; in the natural order there is, at the best,
an ebb and flow in matters of this kind, and recently the
antl-Trea'tmg League has not been so prominent before
the public asit ought to be.  Very many of our readers
are in the way of giving the anti-Treating Movement a

* helpialong the road, and we hope they will do it. Any-

thing tending to take a chip off that awful tribute of
some £14,000,000 is good and wholesome work.

There are two sides to everything ; and we have been
he:_m'ng something of the other side of the drapers’
assistants’ agitation for shorter howrs on Saturday night,
One contention is that early closing on Saturday would
play into the hands of thé Jew traders who carry packs.
The working men could not shop on Monday, and the
Jewman would be round waiting on him during his
dinner hour on Monday. We do not know how much
force there is in this contention ; we put the point as it
was put to us.

. Of cowrse, Saturday night is the great popular spend-
ing night in these countries. Tt is the night of pay-day
in Dublin, and the weekly-wage-earning population
naturally spend a great deal of their money on the day
they get it. If Wednesday was the general pay-day,
probably the popular shops would keep open late on
Wednesdays and close earlier on Saturdays.  Some
classes of workmen are busiest on general holidays;
from the nature of the work tram-men, for instance,
are busiest on days when the majority of people are
making holiday. Drapers who serve the popular de-
mands are naturally busiest when the populace
gets its wages. If the populace is in the Streets
at 10-30, and the drapers’ shop is shut and M.
Bung is all aglow with lights, the result might not be
altogether satisfactory; though on the other hand it
might be urged if certain shops were closed the populace
would not be so much in the Streets and within the
attracting power of Bung on Saturday night. A
great, fact that strikes the ordinary impartial outsider
is that families with very limited incomes derived in
weekly instalments, inevitably tend to spend a large
part of their money on the day they get it, and tram-
men might as well look for a day off on a general
holiday as popular drapers look for a short or even a
normal, day on the working-man’s pay-day. One
popular draper told us that he takes as much money on
Saturday as he does during the other five days, and
further, that on Saturday between the hours of 6 and
11 he takes more than half his day’s takings. Another
point is that many popular drapers have boot depart-
ments; if they close, and those shops that go in ex-
clusively for boots remain open, the drapers’ boot de-
partment suffers from the unfair competition.

Everybody, of course, would wish that a deserving
body like the drapers’ assistants in the popular houses
should get off early on Saturday, bub the difficulty is
that Saturday is naturally the poor man’s purchasing
day. Why not agitate for a half-day in the middle of
the week? We do not anticipate that that would
meet with any formidable opposition from the drapers.
A draper who can say that he does upwards of 25 per
cent, of his week’s trade between the hours of 6 and 11
on Saturday night has a strong case against those who
would ask him to shut at 7 on Saturdays, but would
have a very weak case against a request to shub at 2
o'clock on Wednesday or Thursday. How would it be
if there were a movement in the direction of changing
the working-man’s pay-day from Saturday to Friday?
That, were it suceessful, might relieve the congestion of
Saturday’s buying and smooth the way for moderately
early closing on 'that day. We will welcome the nows

“of the assistants on these matbters.

A Southern correspondent thinks' we have recently
given too much space to the Procession incident. We
an quite understand his point of view, and appreciate
it; but it is not the incident—the conduct of the.roughs

THE LEADER. %

is beneath notice—but what is beside and behind the in-
cident that lends it whatever claim to notice it may
have. The incident will not be ready for burial until
after the next meeting of the Coiroe Snota: when:
the Coipoe Tnota digs a deep pit for it the incident
will be ready for interment, and will afterwards live
only as a memory. In the course of his letter our cor-
respondent, says:—“ You have been a noble pioneer,
and there is still a lot of work to do besides wasting time:
and good space for blackguards. Send an envoy down
South and shake up the Only National—the banks, the
wholesalo and retail trades who wage a silent, but effec-
tive, war against Romish institutions. Let him visit
the shipping companies, and see how the poor Gaels fare
where the ‘Church of Tahland’ gentlemen hold sway.
This will give you something to do and write about in-
stead of wasting space on blackguards like the ‘Lan-
guage incident’ type. I take an interest in the LEADER,
and when necessary will show it in a practical way to-
wards the ‘Daily Leader,” and this fact impels me to
write you in this strain.”

There was no spontaneous apology for the Procession
blackguardism, and no denunciation of it on the part
of the authorities of the Gaelic League. It will be re-
membered that the official organ superciliously re-
marked—“A. correspondent who does not give his name,
has written us to inquire whether the Coipoe JTnota
through an Clardeam Soluip or otherwise, will offer
an apology for an incident which occurred at the Dub-
lin Language Procession, on Sunday week last. The
same question has been asked by one or two corrves-
pondents of the Irish Independent. The answer, of
course, is that the Procession was carried out under the
auspices not of the Co'roe Fnota but of the Dublin
Coiroe Ceanncaip and the matter can come before the
Coipoe Snota only by way of appeal from the decision
of the latter body.” This mixture of Balfourian lan-
guidness and Government Department red tape would
be wholly ludicrous were it not for the light it throws
upon the diplomacy, or lack of it, that has floated to the
surface of the Organisation

A Limerick correspondent, writes:—Gaelic Leaguers
here are very indignant at the way in which you were
treated dwrng the Language Procession in  Dublin.
They have sent on a resolution to the Secretary of the
Gaelic League demanding that the Coipoa Snota dis-
associate themselves and the Organisation from the
mcident,

We continue to receive letters expressing unstinted
condemnation of the blackguardism applauded and en-

dorsed by the “ coufrere ” of the official organ. M.
Jeremiah J. Cronin, of Macroom, who is
so  well known, particularly in connection
with the Town Tenants’ agitation, sends us a

eopy of a letter which he has forwarded to the President
of the Gaelic League. Mr. Cronin, by way of establish-
ing his right to protest, states that he was one of the
first in Macroom to join the Gaelic League, and that as
far back as five years ago he gave one of the organisers
of the League a cheque for £6 for a little advertisement
in the official organ. The letter from: Mr. Jeremiah
Cronin, of Macroom, is a strong protest, and some of it is
so complimentary to ourselves that we would not care to
publish it all in our paper. Here is an extract from it : —
“While I am writing this sentence a gentleman at my
eounter says the LEADER has done more good in Ireland
for the last three years than all the other papers that are
printed in it. I have beard the Very Rev. Canon
Murphy, P.P., D.D., President of our Macroom Gaelic
League, say the LEADER is the best pennyworth sold in
Ireland. I have heard an Irish organiser say within the
past week—Ob, it is a great pity. The Irish would not
be half so good only for the LEapEr.” e winds up his
vigorous ‘letter as follows:—“I know there will be a
public apology to the LEaDER expected from the
Dublin leaders who promoted the procession; and I am
strongly of opinion if some amends are not. made and re-
gret expressed that the Language Collection will be
much smaller next year in the South, which I would
regret.”
Here is a letter from a correspondent in Cork City : —
“Needless to say, you have on your side practically all
the Gaelic League here excepting, of course, the mem-
Ders of the ‘ Green Hungarian Band,” and some few milk-
and-water fellows who do not count. The affair came as
a thunderbolt to us all here, and is being much debated
on all sides. Even those who do not agree with the
policy of the LEADER agree in condemning the persona-
lity." The tactless powers that be at headquarters
are considered here by practically all—Tin men included
—to have -made a great mistake in tactics—everything
else excluded.”
Here are extracts from a letter from the County Wex-
ford:—“As a constant reader (and admirer) of your
journal allow me to give some expression to the indigna-
tion T felt, in common with all your readers at the recent
dastardly outrage perpetrated on you. The indignity it
was sought to pup upon you has fallen on the other side.
By their peurile reply to pertinent criticism these gentry
have writien themsclyves down as asses of a particularly
long-eared variety. If the Gaclic League in Dublin is
going to degenerate into cliqueism and cornerboyism, so
much the worse for the Dublin Gaelic League.”  We
take the following from a letter received from another
part of the County Wexford :— By the way, you have
the sympathy and the sense (best people) of Wexford on
your side re that very regrettable incident in the pro-
cession.” - .

We have seen that red liberty, as individualised by
the ex-Tin Pike organ, turned its blind eye to phe flaring
Union Jack that streamed from a pole stuc'k in a turret
of the Protestant Chapel on the South Circular Road
where an Irish sermon was preached on St. Patrick’s
Day. We read in last week’s isslfe of the Orga.u‘that
doos not like the Catholic Association :— A notice was
issued that the band of his Britannic Majesty’s 88th
Connacht Rangers would give a glravnd musical concert, to
wind up with © God Save the King of England.”” We
understood that Tommy Atkins’ bands usually wound up
with “God Save the King”’ But our one-time rebels
have now become subtle—so subtle that it is impossible
to determine whether they are tin “ rebels” or aca-
demic loyalists; their organ stands for the Kiing's
Most Excellent Majesty, the Lords and Commons of Tre-
land, and therefore it instinctively feels that it is so
much “ God Save the King ” that it has to represent a
Tommy Atkins band as winding up with “ God Save the
King—of England.” The concert. referred to Loo!; I’]-apey
it appears, at Castlebar. We read in the ex-Tin Pike
organ, now the organ of the King’s Most Excellent Ma-
jesty, the Lords and Commons of Ireland, that:— The
concert was a complete failure—financially and otherwise
-.a fact which has disturbed the parish priest. On
Sunday at first Mass the protestors at the * God Save the
King’ Concert were denounced, and the Rev. Canon
Lyons informed the congregation that ‘God Save the
King’ was the National Anthem. The rev. gentleman
also said the British soldiery should be respected in
Castlebar.” Well, if Canon Lyons said that “ God Save
the King "—a tune which we equate in this country with
“Damn the ‘ Idolators’ "—was the ¢ National Anthem,”
we beg to strongly differ with him whatever the loyal
ex-Tin Pike men may say ; but what we want to point out
particularly is that this organ, that does not like the Ca-
tholic Association, finds it easy to denounce a Catholic
Canon for alleged “loyalty” whilst its blind eye is
turned on the flaunting Union Jack that flapped from a
pole emerging from a turret of the Protestant Chapel on
the South Circular Road during the Irish service on St.
Patrick’s Day.

Recently the Ennis Board of Guardians had to elect a
Relieving officer for the district of Killaniv and
Crusheen. There is a standing order on the books of
the Ennis Union, according to which the Guardians
bind themselves to give preference to condidates having -
a knowledge of Irish, other things being equal. From
the report before us, it does not plainly appear whether
any of the seven candidates knew Irish. We quote the
following from a report in a local paper:—“Mr.
Moloney drew attention to the clause in the advertise-
ment requiring candidates to have a knowledge of the
Irish language. Did they know it? A voice—Oh, my
goodness. Mr, Skehan—What good is the Irish lan-
guage? Mr. Moloney—I .can assure you I have no
interest in_any of the candidates one way or the
other, but I would like to be satished if Mr. Daly has
a knowledge of the language so that I could support
him, and I would like him to addvess a few words to
us. Mr, Skehan was understood to say that he had a
knowledge of the Inish language.  Mr. Culligan—
would you please examine them in Irish, Mr. Molony?
The Chairman—There was an order made on the adver-
tisement, and my name is put to it, and I will stand
by it. A guardian at the end of the room made a
remark in Irish, which was translated as, ‘it’s all hum-
bug.” Mr. Molony again pressed for some test as to the
candidate’s knowledge of the language. Mr. Doohan—
I would be in favour of supporting a member of the
United Irish League.” Whether the man elected to the
posh knows Irish or not does not appear from the report:
before us. What is the value of a resolution?

DEVOLUTIONISTS AND DYNAMICS.

N international disputes the side that has the biggest
l_ battalions is prone to think it has all the best
of the argument, and the side with the weak battalions
tends to throw as much of its argument overboard as
will be calculated to stave off a conflict. Facts are
facts, and in this imperfect: world the shrewd observer
of affairs must have had it frequently borne in upon his
mind that when inter-class or international disputes
arise Dynamics rather than Ethics is more often than not
the ruling power. Dynamics—though some may think
it cynical on our part to say so—rather than Ethics
is the element in the boiler that has set the Reform
Association a-bubbling. Landlordism, with its ragged
camp following of place-hunters, peoinin shop-keepers
and imported stable boys is doomed; the mere Irish
backbone is becoming stronger, and raime:s, though
like the poor, it will be always with us, has received a
set-back from the high place it occupied in the Na~
tional  Counsels. A hard-headed, stoutrhearted,
critical and constructive nationality is budding up.
Some Unionists who have brains enough to dimly per-
ceive the scene-shifting that is proceeding are being
stimulated to half-thought by what we may call the
dynamics of the situation.

Now the dynamic basis of the conciliators is a matter
that mere Irishmen should keep constantly in mind
If a man has robbed you of £500 and is beyond your
reach, you would naturally be glad if he showed a
disposition to disgorge £100 or even £50; but if he was
coming within your reach and talked of giving
you back £60 of the £500 that he had stolen from you,
you as a hard-headed man, might say that as he was
coming within your grasp you would not think of com- -
promising and crying quits until the proffered restitm-
tion swelled to £200, or £300 or £400 out of the £500¢
that you had been robbed of. The day of Ascendancy
is doomed, if the mere Irish have the grit to play a
strong hand, though at the present moment, largely
owing to the cowardice—we cannot use a milder term—
of the mere Irish, the Orange faction are calling the
tunes. Some half-shrewd Unionists who see dimly the
trend of events are trimming—they call it conciliation.
Lord Mayo, though as per publicly flaunted advertise-
ment, his steward must be “saved,” is a “Devolu-
{ionist—as a matter of fact he is only an Ascendancy
man in a corner, like many other Devolutionists. Now
that the big babtalions show a tendency to come over
to the side of the mere Irish, the mere Irish ought to
be made conscious of their power. Some of the concilia-
tors are throwing sand out of their balloon, and they
are hoping thab. the poor mere Irish fools, creatures of
halfslavery. and ravmers, W_{ll_think that it is. t.helr
nearts that ave growing warm rather than that their
livers. are turning white. The mere Irish ha}d betite:
go on unheeding in the work of creating a mere

entity with absorbing power. The doing of that work
will “inevitably create Devolutionists and Concilia
tionists and Dunravens. A Devolutionist is several
degrees more hopeful than a true blue “to Hell with the
Pope” Orangeman; but the thing to guard against is

Mayo, whose steward must be “saved,” should not in
any way impede the growth of the mere Ivish back-

At a Devolutionist, meeting at Drogheda, recently,
TLord Louth gave an exhibition of a man in a corner
who cannot make up his mind. We are not inclined to
think that it is his lordship’s political conscience that
is working upon him, but rather that what we may
<all the dynamics of the situation are making his lord-

of one who feels that the garrison is going, and that the
mere Irish are developing into an entity on their own
account: At the same time, he felt himself in a sort

#0 better the situation was concerned, because the two

control of the clamour of extremists, that as had been
said, ‘there was no room for a moderate man.” As a
Unionist and landlord, he could not side with those
who professed a creed the main articles of which seemed
to be the disruption of the Empire; hatred, not only

out of the country of their birth himself and his friends.
On the other hand, as an Irishman, and one who recog-

abuse of three-fourths of his fellow-countrymen, and
whose whole statesmanship never seemed to soar above
“firm government,” which so often in practice meant
coercion.” Men like Lord Louth have not their hearts
in Ireland, they are not—at least yet—of Ireland; but
landlordism is going and they are looking out weakly
and with heavy hearts for some half-way house where
they might get some little welcome from one side, and
be somewhat sheltered from the bolts and brick-bats
©of the other. Now that Ascendancy is tottering to its
fall, men like Lord Louth are weakly retrospective.
Here is a sample:—“In days not long past if a man
ventured to suggest that he did not think the system
of government under which they lived was perfection,
he was howled at by those who called themselves
genuine Unionists as an enemy of the Empire, disloyal
to his King and country, a discredit and a disgrace to
his party and his order. Agitators, it was said, were
the curse of the country. Tt never struck them that
agitators could not exist for a quarter or a half a cen-
tury if there were not at least some sort of foundation
for agitation.”” As long as the world is a world it will
be the part of strong, brave, and pioneering men to
‘have ‘themselves howled down in the cause of Truth
and Progress. A public man who is not howled at is
only a time-server, or an amiable: gentleman popular at
tearparties, and whom nobody is foolish enough to
take seriously. Was it for fear of being “howled at”

that.the Lord Louth’s of to-day allowed. Ethics. in years °

gone by to wait upon Dynamics?

We welcome the change that has manifested itself

should be honest, though his honesty be less a manifes-

should be picking pockets. Devolutionists are more
hopeful than true blue ascendancy bigots. The point
for the mere Irish to keep steadily in view is thatl the

Treland go on working itself into a strong, conquering,
absorbing entity—Dynamics, and no. doubt: a little
‘Ethics and a possible sprinkling of patiiotism may be
4rusted to hasten on the slow evolution of the better-

OUR IRISH GOYERNMENT BOARDS AND
THEIR BLUNDERS,

WAS glad to see that the strictures on the dction
of the Ldcal Goveritment Board and the advice
to the County Councils and Corporations concerned with
the “Public Bodies’ Order” so stiffened their attitude
to the gentlemen at the Custom IHouse, that the
“County Councils' Gencral Council” as representative
of the public elected Boards have met, and in no un-
measured language denounced the conduct and action
of the unrepresentative body who issued *‘ the order.’
With one or two: exceptions, there was not a single
speaker at the County Council’s General Council meet~
ing but denounced the high-handed, if not illegal, action
of the L. G. B., and not only that, but in the most
emphatic manner pointed out blunder after blunder
which that autocratic body has made time after time
in the past. Mr. O'Neill, the Chairman of the Dublin
County Council, in the absence of Sir Thomas Esmonde,
presided, and the whole tenor of his speech showed not
only what an irritating and vexatious policy the L. G
B. have been systematically pursuing for the past six
years, but more pertinently still, pointed out what an
incompetent, expensive, and unbusiness body the gentle-
men who rule from the Custom House are.

It seems' an extraordinary statement to make, bub
it-is nevertheless true; that in the two or three stand-up
fights on matters of expenditure, and procedure, where
the County Council of Dublin has had to appeal against,
the Local Government Board, at a serious expenditure
of money—in one case Parliament had to be appealed
to, in each instance the County Council’s views were
correct, and the L. G. B.’s rulings were irregular and
illegal.  Indeed, in one of the cases cited by Mr.
O’Neill, the L. G. B. actually advised the County
Council to commit an illegal action—knowing it to be
illegal—inr order to cover up, and conceal the wrong
advice: they had already given. As Mr. O'Neill
added, the Council over which he presided had for
the past six years been in constant conflict with the
L. G. B., and with the most complete and ample know-
ledge of the details of these conflicts, he now publicly
declared ‘that it had been in every instance provoked
and goaded into these conflicts by the Board itself.
In fact the County Council had been compelled to
lavishly spend the ratepayers’ money in order to ham-
mer common. sense and some. knowledge of the law into
the wooden heads of the Board and their still more
wooden legal advisers.

In fact, as Mr. O'Neill stated, it was the exception,
not the rule, when the L. G. B. was right, and in
every instance in the matters at issue, the deliberate
intentior of the Board was to curtail and to cripple,
and to minimise and be-little the action of the Coun-
cils, and the locally elected bodies. The Custom House
people weres jealous of the power conferred on. local
bodies. They wished to exercise over them the rod-of-
iron' policy which was in vogue before the' Local Govern-
ment Act was passed. In fach they were determined,
ast far as in them lay, to frustvate, to override and
render inoperative the.intention of Parliament in. their
administration of the Act. But this action of the
L. G. B. is nob new. It has been the deliberate
traditional policy of all Government. Boards in. Ireland,
and not, only Government Boards, but the Judges, the
Law Officers of the Crown, the Resident Magistrates,
the Police, and indeed every Board or Department, that
derives its administrative powers or jurisdiction from
Dublin Castle. The plainest, the simplest, the clearest
expressions of Parliament as embodied in an Act or a
Provisional Order, are twisted, and turned, and wrested
from their ordinary meaning by Judges, Law Advisers,
Magistrates and Police Officers until the evident in-
tention of the original statute is not recognisable. Take
the “Healy Clause” in' Gladstone’s Land Act, take the
Bonus Grant in last year's Land Act, or the directions

. Itis this wresting, this straining of the law—in every

in fa.w_)m‘. of a class, or to retain privileges which Parlia-
ent in its wisdom has passed on to other hands, that
bnll_g_s.hlle law into contempt in Ireland, and those who
-administer it into disrepute ; and in the case of Public
Departments, into constant conflict with popular
-Opinion and with the elected bodies who represent it.

I was glad to see the County Councils Geneyal Coun-
cil took immediate steps to carry into action the decision
arrived at by them to contest the order. They at once
waited on the Custom House people, and pub their case
as representing all the elected Public Bodies in an
-emphatic and forcible manner before the Local Govern-
ment Board Commissioners, with the result that I be-
lieve little more will bo heard of the threatenings and
“sealed orders” that were in store for ‘the locally
-elected Boards if they did not cairy out the impossible
Order.

In the brief account of the interview which took
place between the Deputation and Sir Henry Robinson,
the Vice-President of the Board, as recorded in the
Press, we have it that Sir Henry had to admit much of
ithe Order was difficult to cary out. I understand,
however, from one of the deputation who was present,
that Sir Henry was fairly cornered by Mr. O'Neill, who
bitterly complained of the want of courtesy and that
-common understanding and sympathy that should ob-
fain between the Board and the public elected bodies
in a grave proposed change of this character. For in-
stance, if any of the officials connected with the Asy-
lum’s Boards had been consulted, the absurd, if not
Taughable regulations attempted to be enforced, would
never have been heard of. Several of the rules, as was
pointed out, could not be enforced or carried out, and
Sir Henry admitted they were impracticable. He

! «climbed down considerably, and even went so far as to
| :suggest, that his Board had no desire to harass or
- annoy the public elected Boards, which showed great

condescension on his part. Of course Sir Henry took
i the line which heads of Public Boards and Ministers
always take when cornered, and are unable to meet their
dnterviewers arguments. He would not promise to with-
" «draw the Order. He would like their views on paper—in
.black and white—meanwhile he would endeavowr to
aneet, their ideas as far as possible, and so on, and so on.

I think, however, little more will be heard of the
famous Order, and the new books and costly set of
Hforms, and the one hundred and one regulations which
were to be enforced in draconian fashion from the first
-of April—rather an ominous day!—are gone by the
‘board. I trust the public bodies who have already
B .signified their intention of going on with the old books

.and- forms, and who announced they will not force their
:Secretary or Chief Clerk, or indeed empower them at
G _ .all, to send up the minutes and the other minute de-
tails which were ordered in the famous Custom House
. -edict, will stick to their guns. The Local Government
Board officials wanted to be taught a lesson, and they
‘have got it. They will think twice before they attempt
“to, send down ‘“‘sealed orders” in the face of the volume
-of public opinion which has been aroused over this ill-
sconceived measure. Mr. Walter Long, our new
‘Chief- Secretary, in the Iouse of Commons with
:a bold front, still says the Board at the Custom House
will not withdraw the Order. Well, no one wants them
<to withdraw the “Order.” It has not yeb been put in
~ ‘force, and until a thing is enforced it cannot be wibth-
«drawn. It may be the Chief Secretary’s playful way
+of hinting that it will not be enforced. -
The Local Government Board, without inténding it,
‘have done much good. They have welded public
pinion in Ireland, as expressed through its elected
‘Boards, into form and into a great force. They have

THE LEADER

focussed on this question men’s ideas and a mass of
public opinion as to the system of mis-government by S
theso wretched bureaucratic Boards, which under other
circumstances it would have been difficult to collect and
hammer into shape. As long as the Custom House people
only attacked one Board here and a Council there, and
only worried some poor Board of Guardians, or some
small corporate body elsewhere, they were able to carry
on their nagging, and worrying, and irritating policy
with much more ease, though not with impunity, for
the Dublin County Council and the Wexford County
Council have already given them some nasty falls. Still
some general measure which attacked the whole of the
public elected bodies, was needed to bring this system
of nag and worry to a crisis, and to show the L. G. Bt
officials that the times have changed. The old order of
brow-beating and hustling has gone by, and public
opinion in Ireland, as shown through its elected Boards,
will tolerate these ascendancy antics no longer. The
Local Government Board have sought for a lesson how
far public spirit would bear with them, and they
have had it, but not quite in the manner they expected
it. What Government Board’s turn will it be next?

AN ENGLISHMAN AT HIS POST.

By night and by day I ever ever pray
This Irish language may to pieces go.
I never will forget the woes that I met

Through the Gaels at the G.P.O.
West British Ballads.

“Charge, Chester, charge! On, Stanley, on!” cried the
dying Marmion on Flodden Field, nearly four hundred
years ago. To-day the words are slightly changed, and
the cry goes “Charge, Egerton, Charge! On, Stanley, on !”
among the royal mailed warriors of the General Post
Office in their present war against Ireland. For the
latest fortress of British Ascendancy to don the coat of
royal mail against Ireland is the General Post Office.
The General Post Office is situated in “Sackville” Street
in close and appropriate proximity to England in the
shape of Nelson, so on one side we have the Pillar of
British Ascendancy, and on the other the Post. No
doubt some West British seer gifted with second sight,
could, on glancing from Post to Pillar, see the memor-
able signal flying over the head of the great Horatio
of Trafalgar, “England expects that every man this day
will do his duty.” The commanding officer of the Post
is General Horatio Egerton Stanley Dump, one of Eng-
land’s hearts of oak, who bwrns with a martial zeal to
cover himself with glory against the Irish, the same as
Horatio on the Pillar did against the French. Although
Horatio at the Post, unlike Horatio on the Pillar, can
seo prebby clearly with both optics, he affects to be
stone blind so far as Irish signals are concerned, for
whenever a Gaelic inscription’ looms up before him he
looks at it with sightless eye, the same as his model on
the Pillar viewed another signal at Copenhagen on one
memorable day. When, in pursuance of the wise old:
‘West, British governmental policy of the survival of the
unfittest, Horatio Egerton came across the Irish Sea
to command the Post, he, perhaps, thought that Ireland

was proof against that terrible onslaught, and in the
end the mailed warriors were literally overwhelmed at
their post. In vain General Dump urged them on with
cries of ‘Temember Trafalgar;” the enemy was de-
moralised and knocked out: of sorts, and they stampeded
from their post leaving the tables strewn with dead
lebters. When Horatio Egerton saw his warriors re-
treating post haste, he turned from his Post to the
Pillar, and said sadly:—There are more things in
Treland here, Horatio, than are dreamt of in our Eng-
land.” With these words General Horatio Egerton

Gaels.

CRITICS AND TﬂE SUPERSENSITIVE.

< I N O criticise is not necessarily to condemn. To point

out the defects which require remedy, is not to
enrol oneself among the opponents of those whose mis-
takes it is sought to rectify. These may appear empty
platitudes, yet in Ireland they are not so, for whosoever
raises his voice to point out what he considers an error
in the conduct of those with whom he is, in the main
in agreement, forthwith is treated as an enemy, and,
what is worse, as a traitor. The 7ole of the candid
friend is not an enviable one, and we know that the
critic is ever met with the rebuke that it is easier to
criticise than to construct. ~ While admitting these
facts it should not be overlooked that critic and candid
friend are alike indispensable and to treat them as ill-
intentioned is to treat them unfairly. “What is truth?”
said Jesting Pilate, and he might be answered that in
Ireland truth is that which you must not tell your
friends. The man who has the courage to lift his voice
in disapproval of certain schemes or methods does so
at the risk of being thrust forth into the outer darkness
as a betrayer of the cause he has at heart. Hence we
find very many acquiescing in proceedings to which they
are in reality opposed, thinking to preserve peace and
thus of the two evils choose the less.

Human nature, the world over, is much the same.
and the foregoing paragraph has, therefore, a very
general application. This distaste to criticism, how-
ever, is an unmistakable characteristic of Irish life. The
result is detrimental to our national welfare. Fear
of the consequences of criticism, fear even of the term
critic, tends to suppress free expression of opinion, and
gives .support to small groups of persons who seek to
tyrannise over others. Healthy comment on public
affairs even when unflattering, is much to be desired,
and to reduce to silence all save those who praise is to
beget rottenness in public life. It may be urged that
criticism in Ireland tends to bitterness and personalities.
This is, unfortunately, a well-founded charge. We
Irish are too much inclined to shoot envenomed darts.
There is a tendency, no doubt, to attack men rather
than methods, to impute unworthy motives without
sufficient, cause, to abuse rather than to remonstrate.
This evil, perhaps, is itself an outcome of that super-
sensitiveness already alluded to when utterance of the
word 1in season is certain to result in a death-sentence ;.
why surely it is better to hang for a sheep rather tham
for a lamb!

The deplorable incident at the recent Language Pro-
cession affords a very striking illustration of this na-
tional characteristic of super-sensitiveness. A widely-
read organ of public opinion, since its inception some
years back, one of the strongest and most influential
supporters of the Gaelic League, considers certain pro-
ceedings of that body open to objection and proceeds to:
criicise them. Whether that criticism is of the qualit;
to which I have already referred is a question which
I shall readily be pardoned for not entering upon, but
be that as it .may, we find this paper immediately
attacked, hostility engendered against it where there is

teachers as seriously as ever the vested interests of any
class of public servants were threatened by an official
rule. I refer, of course to Rule 127(b) of the Com-
missioners’ New Code which forbids the enrolment of
children under eight years of age in any school in
which a mistress is not employed. ~This is a rule which
- in one of its aspects ought, I think, to be discussed by
 teachers with considerable caution. I don’t know
‘whether it was any part of the design of those who
med it to cast in a bone of contention between men
d women teachers and thus create a split in the

_obvious that the situation which has been created is

an Organisation, the cataclysm is imminent.

It seems to me that the rational and safe attitude for
the men teachers to take up on this matter is to
insist on the rights of individual parents to decide the
question as far as their own children are concerned.
If a panticular parent thinks that the neighbouring
Girls’ School is a better and more suitable place for
his little boy of six or seven than the neighbouring
Boys' School, then by all means let him have full
liberty to send him there; bub, if on the other hand, he
feels satisfied from his knowledge of the schools and
teachers that the boy will be better off in the Boys
School, then let him have the same liberty to act on
his judgment. I do not think any teacher, lady or
gentleman, will dispute the reasonableness of this. If
those of us who happen to be parents will bring the
matter home to ourselves, we will, I think, at once
recognise the absurdity and injustice of fettering the
parental discretion in the manner which the rule pro-
poses to do.

Passing, however, from this aspect of the case alto-
gether, we are face to face with the fact that if the
rule as it stands is enforced, thousands of teachers will
be either thrown out of employment altogether, or will
suffer loss of income or degradation in rank in greater
or less degree. The figures are not available to show
the exact extent of the havoc that will be caused, but
we all know that it will be tremendous. It appears
that the fine Cromwellian spirit which dictated the rule
is already beginning to fail its authors in the face of
the hurricane of public indignation which they have
raised about their ears. It is announced that two-
teacher schools will not be changed into one-teacher
schools by the operation of the rule, which
means that about 1,000 assistants are to escape eviction
for the present at all events. I don’t know why it is
that teachers were left to find out by private interviews
that this had been decided on. Wouldn't it have been
better if it had been stated as an addendum to the
rule Do the Commissioners think it good for Irish
education that the teachers should be kept in a per-
petual state of apprehension and alarm?

No promise of a re-assuring character appears to have
been made as regards the assistants in schools with
three or more teachers; and “special consideration’
for individual cases appears to be the only thing in the
way of comfort offered to the thousands of Principals
who will suffer in salary and capitation by the diminu-
tion of their attendance. I think the teachers should
be warned by the past as to what “specal consideration”
means. They know that up to the present it did not
mean that a claim would be allowed because it was in-
trinsically just, but that if the manager was a man of
strong will and great perseverance, he might, perhaps,
get; it listened to and procure the few pounds increase
of salary by dint of applications to the office, questions.
in the House of Commons, and perhaps letters in the
newspapers. They must insist and they will, if they
are not fools, insist here and now, that if this rule is
not withdrawn it must be qualified by other rules as
specific and as binding as itself, providing the fullest: :
protection for every vested interest down to the last ol
farthing’s worth which it imperils.

For the past twenty-five years or more, the teacheis
have not been in as good a position to make a fight
upon any issue as they are now. The managers are
on their side; the Catholic Bishops as far as they have
spoken are on their side; the public sentiment of the
country is on their side. I venture to think that these
forces will prove too strong for the Commissioners of
National Education who have no one on their side—
no one but their own twenty-one selves.

7Y HE Directors of the Opera House know their public

well, and, it would appear, are prepared to supply
that public with whatever it demands, be that wholesome
or the other thing.  Yet the public must sometimes be
protected against itself; all its demands are not to be
supplied. It will not pass for you to tell me that there
is a demand for this or that. ~ A certain type of mind
will crave for smutty literature, but liberal as the law of
the land is, it draws the line. There is a type of mind,
too, that revels in such immoralities as “ When London
Sleeps,” but surely there is some moral responsibility on
those who provide these plays. Any amount of wrig-
gling cannot shift the responsibility. I do not want to
labour the point further, but I have just to remind the
Opera House Directors on the words in which they were
summed up some, not very long, time ago by that strong
man whose memory must still be with them. Unfortu-
nately for us we have in our midst too few of such men ;
indeed, when all is said and done the atmosphere is not
favourable to their production.

Owing mainly to the lack of grit in most of our Catho-
lics of power and influence, our religious practices, and
occasionally our ministers, become a fair mark for the
scoff and the sneer of some of our “superior” visitors.
The Catholic, in fact, would reply to these visitors whe-
ther they come with the Bible and the Sunday chanting
in the market-place or on the stage; but the spirit of
grovel has possessed us to-such a pitch that verily we
know not when it is cowardice not to strike out from the
shoulder. Let what may be said, the amount of ' real
backbone being developed is miserably small; it is only
when the test is applied that the actual flabbiness may
be gauged. Talk and noise will not do; I should pre-
fer, for propagandist work, if I may put it, one bite to
all the barking. Depend upon it when all is analysed
to its final, it is only the bite that will be found of effi-
cacy. Whenever I hear a body of men resolve, speech-
make, and disband my experience tells me that the
verbal movement, has begun and ended there. “We
hereby, in meeting assembled,” is just the safety valve,
and the oration is the steam. Unless the units can, and
will, think each for himself, and with resolute decision,
we will continue our zigzag kind of progress. Let the
energies be reserved, not frittered away in worse than
useless speeches and resolutions. Day after day the oc-
casions for protest arise; how rarely upon these occasions
do we find our Irish Irelanders at their posts, or, if at
their posts, ready for action.

“YOUNG IRELAND” AND IRISH IRELAND.

g PON the subject of our Anglo-Irish Ballads T

have nothing to add to what Mr. Duffy has so

truly and so ably written. That there is a distinct

character and peculiar charm in the best ballads of this

class, which the highest genius, unaccompanied by

thorough Irish feeling, and a thorough Irish education,

would fail to impart to them, must be evident to every-

one who has read that volume. To those among us, and

to the generations who are yet to be among us, whose

mother tongue is, and of necessity must be, the English

and not the Irish, the establishing of this fact is of the

3 utmost importance (hear hear), and of the greatest con-

3 solation (hear, hear); that we can be thoroughly Irish

3 in our feelings without ceasing to be English in our

speech (hear, hear), and that we can be faithful to the

land of our birth without being ungrateful to the

literature which has been ‘the nursing mother of our

minds’ (applause); that we can develop the intellectual

resources of our country, and establish for ourselves a

distinct and separate existence in the world of letters

without depriving ourselves of the advantages of the

widely-diffused and genius-consecrated language of Eng-

land, are facts that I conceive cannot be too widely
disseminated” (hear, hear, and applause).

The above, without the interjected applause, is from
the preface to D. F. MacCarthy's “Irish Ballads,”
1845. I have put in the applause just about where any
of our Irish Literary Societies would put it in if the
piece were read to them in meeting assembled. Any
? Gaelic Leaguer that likes can try it. He can start off

with the quotation as an opening to a paper on, say,
“Young Ireland, old and new.” When he has verified
about the applause, and feels his audience softening in
their seats in expectation of hearing their silently
cherished ideas played to, he will be in all the better
humour to talk to them for their good.

It would be idle to try to classify in any minuteness
the Young Ireland of to-day as per the Societies it
dawdles its leisure hours in. Indeed, the great bulk
probably doesn’t bother about any Society. A rough
division of the remainder that does enrol itself would
be into the mere timekillers—the Young Men’s Socie-
ties and the like—and the will-be actives. As far as
TIrish Treland is concerned, the majority first mentioned
—those who don’t belong to any Society—are only wait-
ing to be spoken to and enthused to fall into line. Of
the others, the time-killers, are too supremely happy as
they are to be moved for years yet. There remain the

- will-be actives. These include the various shades of
Society—Irish Literary, National and Literary, Celtic
Literary, and so on—from that type that holds ad-
journed debates on the Shakespeare-Bacon controversy
- to that other most sturdy of the types, Cumman na
ngaeveal whose members aspire to lift the whole poli-
tical life of the country and to direct it. From the
point, of view of the Irish-Ireland army picquetting for
recruits, these will-be actives all afford ready material,
indeed the more alive of them. have already enlisted.
They make useful skirmishers. They require, however,
to be put under stern discipline and taught to distin-
ish the colours, else they are likely to lead the main

body into unexpected and unpleasant situations.

The current issue of the New Ireland Review is one of
the best all round issues of that monthly periodical
that we have seen for a long time. The opening
article, “Hatred within the United Kingdom,” by
Arthur Synan, makes interesting reading, and M.
‘William Dawson’s article on “The Irish Layman and
the Catholic Church,” is a bold treatment of a subject,
which is as important as it is delicate; it will probably
give rise to a discussion, and the discussion could not
e carried out: in a better medium than the New Ireland
Review. Father T. A. Finlay’s article on “The Position
of Teachers in our Primary Schools,” is a masterly and
rather exhaustive effort on this great question of live
importance at the present moment. = The financial
slump into which the new arrangements will tend to
drive teachers who have entered the teaching profession
under the “National” Board since April 1st, 1900, is
set forth with great clearness and emphasis.  This
article has a general interest for all who follow Irish
affairs, but it has a particular interest for the teachers
who come within the full scope of the new arrangements
made in 1900, and the latter should make it their
special business to read it.

It seems absurd to consider that a man who takes a
train or a tramcar for a three mile journey in order to
lounge in a public-house and drink during prohibited
hours—it seems absurd to consider such a man a bona
fide traveller. Yet of such type are a large number of
the “honas” who infest such places as Blackrock. Two
*“bonas” went on “ pleastive ” to Blackrock on a recent.
Sunday and turned into a drink-shop.  According: to
ovidence they went into the drink-shop at 8 o’clock in
the evening ~and left at 9.50—that is for a perfod of
nearly two hours these two “ hona fide travellers” rested
their weary limbs and refreshed themselves in a Black-
rock drink-shop: This brace of “bona fide travellers”
walked direct to the railway station on leaving the bun-
gery. At the Kingstown Police Court Mr. Swifte fined
these weary, foot-sore and travel-stained “ Bonas” a sum
of 10s. each. Mr Swifte said to Superintendent Talbot
—I suppose this prosecution is to put a stop to the prac-
tice of men coming from Dublin to Blackrock to obtain
drink ; and Mr. Talbot replied—Yes, your worship. We
want to stop these.men coming out here and staying in a
public-house for an hour and a half drinking. = The
police authorities in this enterprise will have the hearty
good will of every right-thinking person in the commu-
nity.
